item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Pexels changed its license from Public Domain to a non-compliant license on July 5 2018. The image was uploaded on January 4 2018, predating the license change by six months, and thus is permitted on the wiki. It is licensed under Public Domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Stumbling across this series while going through the dado hub felt the emotional equivalent of plunging a hot can into an ice bath! I suppose that's the SCP wiki for you though. I've always loved stories dealing with "fates worse than death", but I've never seen something that looks at the concept in such a detailed, expansive way.

+ Spoilers
- Spoilers

Relating to 2718, I initially thought it mirrored the experiences of the people in this scenario closely, but on a reread the O5 still had a distinct "point" of death and retained full awareness of his body (which doesn't seem to occur here). My interpretation is that the 05 remained tethered to the physical world due to the nature of the resurrection procedure, or perhaps due to the very fact that it would occur.

I'm also curious about what the motivations are behind the death entity. It's implied that it creates an afterlife echoing the subjects fears, but what would be the reasons for doing so? An ever-burning hatred of humanity? Or maybe it's a neutral entity, but the human consciousness defaults to fear in a post-death state naturally? Maybe there will be answers in season 2, I'll definitely be diving into that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't that "the price is paid" phrase refer to SCP-106 containing Roger in his pocket dimension eternally?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure how I feel about this one. SCP-2718 had a lot of mystery, and I'm glad that this skip manages to capture the right intentions and concepts of 2718.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't this kind of imply that death is pretty much universally a living hell? It kind of takes the weight off of an end of death scenario if there was never any peace to be had in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doublepost because now I'm confused. Why was Tony's motivation his father doing that hike? Didn't he know his father had Alzheimer's? Killing the Reaper for a selfish reason was bad enough, but for an impossible one is even worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the end, this series redeemed 2718 to me. It's like 2718 was an early draft, or even a foreshadow of 3448.

Incredible job, you guys.

Wow… It hasn't even been a year yet and this puppy has already hit 300. I'm happy you guys all enjoyed it this much, and the content that surrounds it.

To another year of immortality! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, that was a good ride. About as atmospheric as SCP-4000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just got done with the first part of this incredible series, cant wait to binge read the rest! Made me re-evaluate my whole fearful outlook on death, and genuinly sent chills down my spine every few paragraphs. Amazing work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Local secret organization ruins everything.

item.title: Re: Loved every single bit of it
item.content:encoded: How do those compare with, say, a typical novel, novella, or print-published SF short story?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't give any intelligent comment on this meisterwerk… I'm just slighty amused by how the way to "kill" death is to… symbolically destroy a pistol, and then shoot her in the face with it, in limbo-land…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah … essentially the cells themselves aren't really the root of intelligence or consciousness (though the latest research around the connectome may yet prove otherwise), instead it's the pattern of signals that are being communicated between them… Which may have just found the grey matter a nice convenient place to sit, but ultimately don't actually require it to survive in an intact form to persist…

item.title: Oh Death, Where is your sting?
item.content:encoded: Amazing imagery there. Absolutely Amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
EDIT: Hold on, No.

SCP-2817 isn't caused by 3448. It was being prevented by Death, until the Foundation interfered. Rogers is right. 2817 is simply our natural fate. Death was an anomalous entity that took pity on us and used its power to override our natural immortality, giving us relief after biological death. By making the 001 who is merged with the conceptof death incapable of perceiving the O-5, they made themselves ressurectable, but also outside of Death's power. They started project Dammerung because they assumed the 2817 hell was caused by the uncontained anomaly called Death. But in reality, she was the only thing preventing it.

But isn't Omega-Kay the cause of 2718? When you decompose while conscious, as millions did, eventually your brain's individual cells will decompose and you will feel everything. When Roger came back, he brought memories from the far future, which caused the Foundation to try to contain Death, which caused the Omega-Kay…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have spent the past three days reading the end of death series, and I think I've been through it all about five or six times now. There were a few parts which didn't seem to follow logic, as some have pointed out, but overall I am fascinated by this entire set.

I stumbled across 3448 on accident (random page) and then hit the hub. The deeper it got, the more drawn in I became. I just wish there was more.

+Spoilers
-Hide

For example, I wish there was additional detail into a few parts, such as the decomposition aspect. Yes, the maggot treatment bit touches on it, but I would like to know what has become common practice in the post omega-k world for people who can't afford body transplants or the sleeping pills once things get bad enough. Do they just leave grandma lying in bed as a skeleton with a brain? Also, the brains which have become separated from their bodies but are still conscious…are they just dreaming or is there some sort of actual awareness as to their surroundings and situation (I'm looking at the landfill brains on this one…)?

Also, I feel something else needs to be elaborated on as to what happened with Tony after killing death. Further communication from him wherever his consciousness is, etc. It seems this is a possible future tale, as Emily was taking Eric to see him. I would love to see what happens from there.

And about Emily…she shot herself and caused massive brain damage, allegedly. How is she just "fine" now? Did I miss something there? Everyone else who had severe blood loss, suffocation, etc, all had irreversible brain damage as a result and were unable to function normally anymore. Same with the beheaded D-class. If this seems like a jumbled mess I apologize, I just have a lot of questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Y'know, I pitched a tale idea like that to Kirby in chat (because of that 3984 test); that the O5 in 2718 doesn't have a recollection of his time dead, but rather, a prophecy of what some may face in this scenario: the smallest fragments of a destroyed yet living mind experiencing ultimate and indescripable pain in the absence of perceiving…well, anything at all.

Ergo, What Happens After is not a promise of what awaits the dead, but a warning from Death Itself of the alternative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Building on this, I headcanon that if you destroyed individual brain cells even further, you could wind up with potentially completely separate atoms that are still technically conscious.

…which kind of brings us back to SCP-2718?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See, it's not that the brain cells are indestructible per se. It's that the electrical activity that one associates with thought continues at all times. Incinerating the cells might degrade their physical properties, but since "thought" still persists the people aren't really dead. That's what they're getting at.

It's not explicitly stated, but it was demonstrated in the last testing log of SCP-3984 with the suspended solution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot to like here. I thought this series had some of the best characterization I've read so far in the contest, and of course, the overall concept is interesting, for reasons others have already said better than I could.

I had to sleep on my vote, because there's one thing, one throwaway line out of all of this, that doesn't sit right with me. It's during "The Foundation Wants a Robot Army" where Greene wants to shoot the "extra" brains into the sun, and is then mocked, because "obviously that wouldn't work," apparently. Young says they've tried incineration, and that there was still electrical activity in the cells.

What this tells me is that not only are brain cells infinitely self sustaining, (which I can get behind, because the reasons for that are explained) but they're apparently invincible to the point where they can withstand incineration and apparently the crushing pressures at the center of a star. And that's just glossed over. If the individual brain cells are this impervious and cant be destroyed, why isn't Prometheus Labs or MC&D taking processed grey matter and coating tanks with it?

Yes, ultimately it's a minor thing across the whole scope of the work, but it's super immersion breaking to me that the pure durability of brain cells isn't addressed more. I like everything else about this story, but I have to go with a no-vote here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm extremely torn on this, as the final twist and tie back to the Michaels family was absolutely excellent, but it was a big let down to see the conclusion of a series I've largely enjoyed rely so much on a tie in to possibly my least favorite article on the wiki in 2718. The disturbing afterlife scenes were also done better in the Corbenic skips imo, though the personification of death's nature was a highlight those didn't have that this one does. Novote for now, I'm going to have to think hard about this one.

Edit: Ultimately, my dislike of 2718 and this climax's failure to account for some of the earlier plot irregularities about Michaels and Young have lead me to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is kind of a mess of horror cliches - being attacked by swarms of insects, creepy dolls, little girls, teddy bears and so on. At a few points it required suppression of eye-rolls, like when fire and spooky skeletons appeared.

I would have preferred to see something more simple in the central logs. However, the pacing is good and I like how this neatly wraps up and puts a bow on the whole scenario, which was enough to +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the compliment, I'm glad you enjoyed!

I don't even mind that it's heavily inspired by one of my least favorite SCPs

This was probably one of my bigger fears when writing this. It requires people to suspend their disbelief that the Foundation would consider death to be an anomaly, which has only been demonstrated in 2718, which is fairly well rated, but also received some backlash. I didn't want to tie this article too closely to that one, but it was a necessary evil. I'm happy that the piece worked well regardless of the connection!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now I'm just glad that we CAN die! Amazing series, this has to get in top 3's for sure. Great job.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The way things build and build is nuts; what starts out relatively middling ends up improving upon itself in wonderful strokes of visual imagery and escalation of events. I don't even mind that it's heavily inspired by one of my least favorite SCPs when you take that concept and put your own spin on it so well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly one of the coolest articles I've seen.

The use of symbolic imagery was impeccable. I'd love to see a series of videos based on the imagery someday. It sounded like something out of tribetwelve or marblehornets, as far as the video editing goes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this spin on the Dämmerung concept.
The video logs feel to me, as if David Lynch collaborated with Salvadore Dali - and conveying that just by words with little to no images is an impressive feat.

And as silly as it sounds: The reason for the whole OmegaK Scenario being the death of the reaper Comes off as plausible to me. And I love the irony that the Foundation itself caused a K Scenario (without playing lolfoundation).

As for the many insects in the "Halfterlife": Are they just there for horror/Limbo/atmosphere aspects or are they the bugs that were retroactively killed by the Anti-Baby Gas and retroactively/in the past sent to the afterlife en masse? (That's what my headcanon was ;) )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I NEED THE LAST STORY. Plus, the whole irony of killing death has hit me. Who takes death when death dies?

item.title: Re: Loved every single bit of it
item.content:encoded:
Ok… because you brought up 093 I got curious about some things.

Warning: Word Counts Beyond This Point
– hide block

093 (with all the supplementary material and everything) comes out to about 14,170 words.

This article (which I believe is the longest of our articles) is only around 5,000 words, so its like a third of 093.

Buuuut the entire series (not including supplementary material, just the articles in the "main story") come out to a grand total of 22,146 words.

And when you do include the supplementary material - its 34,333 words.

Just thought the stats were somewhat interesting.

item.title: Loved every single bit of it
item.content:encoded:
I just wanna say, this is the first time i find myself reading long articles without losing interest since the Red Sea Object. The pacing in writing on every article and how all the pieces fall together is perfectly executed. It's a +1 for me simply based on the fact that pulling this off in so many articles by so many different authors while still making sense is an exceptional feat in of itself.

I'm just sad that it's over, i want more of it!
Thanks everyone for reading! I'm glad you all enjoyed :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean… technically you read the beginning. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good shit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, it's comments like this that reaffirm that my writing did its job.

you guys managed to take my worst fear, subvert it, turn it on its head, and make me feel sorry for not wanting to die.

My first thought when I heard this pitched to me by Othello was "omg this is practically the inverse apocalypse", which I love. I adore subversion in articles, and essentially predicating the entire theme on a subversion is like my entire aesthetic. It feels too easy to try to run a grandiose battle predicated on rule of cool for this topic. Sure, that's a good way to take it, and it can feel original if done right, but I love delving into more nuanced themes.

Also, something appeals to me about an end of the world with no explosions, no high octane action, no violence. Just a very peculiar situation, and everyone just have to live with it.1

Footnotes
1. Pun intended

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddamn it I read the end of the entire series so now I have to read it all!

Though, this is a good thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man.

This may get a bit far, so bear with me.

I am terrified of death. It's by far my greatest fear. Beyond any shadow of a doubt.

If nothing else, your tales have replaced that dread with an even worse one: Not dying. I don't know whether or not to hyperventilate, or just stare at a wall, after finishing everything you guys wrote.

I am very rarely horrified anymore, and you guys managed to take my worst fear, subvert it, turn it on its head, and make me feel sorry for not wanting to die. You fucking animals.

I love you. I hate you. You are all terrible people who have terrified me. You are all the best people.

You are all and none of these.

Enjoy your +1's, because jesus fucking shitfuck you earned them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So when's season 2?
Love it, bit weird, but love it.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. Bravo to all of you - this is among, if not the best material I've read on the site so far. You guys killed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm also on the side of the discussion who believed that 2718 was a unique experience. I actually coined a term for it: "spiritual anchorage". I am fairly sure that Roger's key and secret phrase had something to do with his experience, and possibly caused his soul to anchor to his body to allow for reanimation, meaning he was connected with every cell. Had the O5 council decided not to panic like children, or try to cover it up, they might have been able to test and determine the truth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There should probably be a link to 2718 near the beginning, since it's pretty important to the story.

So, I don't have that crosslink because what matters is that there is a 2718-like anomaly acknowledged by the Foundation. However, I don't want to marry this article to the "rules of the afterlife" implied by 2718.

I tried to set the article up such that it doesn't matter exactly why death is an anomaly, or the exact nature of death's anomalous properties. All the reader needs to understand is that the Foundation is trying to contain death. Although, I may change my opinion on this and add that link if I think it doesn't quite conflict with what we want death to be.

Also thanks for the compliment! Teamcons and best cons! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't quite put my finger on it, but this had a very David Lynch feel to it, and I loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's important to note that 2718 only implies that O5-11's fate is the fate of everyone, but never proves it, and there's significant reason to doubt that. Most notably, if you highlight the space just below the log you'll get this: Ρωγερ, έχετε καταβληθεί τιμή, σοι μετατίθημι στον παράδεισο. According to its SCP declassified page, that translates to 'Roger, your price is paid. I transport you to paradise'. So that line alone means that even if Roger Sheldon's fate is universal, it's not eternal. It's a kind of purgatory.

Plus, if you combine that with info from the rest of the wiki, it doesn't seem like O5-11's fate is universal or even all that common. It's my personal head canon that Roger's body was turned into a soul trap, either because of the quantum retrocausal method of resurrection, or maybe someone just cursed him because he was an asshole who deserved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, no joke, we were considering a Season 2 at some point, so we'll keep those ideas in mind should we or anyone else decide to pursue said season 2 ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There should probably be a link to 2718 near the beginning, since it's pretty important to the story.

+ show block
– hide block

This has been a good series, and this is a pretty good conclusion.

It's not the most surprising conclusion — some people who've read 2718 probably could have predicted the broad strokes of this from the instant someone said "Dammerung" — but it's still interesting seeing how it played out.

Young's character receives more development here than elsewhere, appropriately making earlier articles deeper in retrospect. You get to understand a bit better why she acted the way she did in 3984, which is good.

I do wish there was more of a conclusion for Joyce and Eric, maybe even the D-class from 3984. Their plotlines get left a bit hanging, since we don't know how they process the news or what they do with the information. As well, we know the Foundation's general strategy moving forward, but it'd be nice to see that crystallize in a more definite form than just being a planned strategy. Those things might make for a more complete and satisfying conclusion. If that's not planned for now, I'd love to see the plotline be returned to after the contest is over.

Overall, this has been a great story. The sheer volume of work is incredibly impressive, and each page does a good job of filling out and exploring the world and its implications in a creative way.

Great job, all of you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fantastic. A fitting end and beginning to one of the best series I've ever seen. Thank you guys so much for writing this wonderful thing, so that we all could read it. I absolutely adore the level of detail you all put into your entries, which everything tying together again in the end. I tip my silly hat to you, sirs and madams.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So let me get this straight, because this is a lot to take in.

Spoilers
Spoiled

Basically they had people experiencing O5-11s death from SCP-2718 without actually being dead, and had them somehow control their experiences in order to try and contain death, but ended up shooting her in her face instead? Does this mean all the legions of ancient dead are actually dead now and at peace instead of suffocating endlessly for the rest of forever?

item.title: Re: Yes. This is It. 13 articles later, and we finally ran out of material.
item.content:encoded: This is beautiful and I love you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because the massive DAMMERUNG header was too subtle :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Site-2718

I wonder what that is referencing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Although… might want to through than in a "spoilers" collapsible there bud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ spoilers
– hide block

We fucked up.

We didn't contain death.

We neutralized it.


Ohohohoho, for that line, you deserve my upvote.

item.title: Yes. This is It. 13 articles later, and we finally ran out of material.
item.content:encoded:
It's a competition about the end of the world. Seems fitting to finish at the beginning.

Thanks to Jazstar, stormbreath, not_a_seagull, fieldstone, Sir Baubius, and PeppersGhost for crit.

And a huge shout out to my teammates - OthellotheCat, Veiedhimaedhr, and Croquembouche. Best competition partners a Captain could ask for. I don't think I've met a group of more talented and enthusiastic writers, especially given how new they are to the site (relative to the rest of the field).

Obligatory Author Post Meme
Us while writing for this contest

Image sources:

bonfire - https://www.pexels.com/photo/bonfire-photo-776113/
daisies - https://commons.m.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Daisies_at_Ventnor_Botanic_Garden.JPG
MRI - https://www.marines.mil/Photos.aspx?igphoto=10864

Both the banner and the Project Dammerung icon were created by Croquembouche, with the use of a CC picture:
MGalloway (WMF) CC BY-SA 3.0, from Wikimedia Commons
